seasonal_tips.0: January: Plan your spring garden layout — New Jersey Tea pairs beautifully with Little Bluestem and Black-Eyed Susan.
seasonal_tips.1: February: Still dormant — perfect time to pick your planting spot. Remember: good drainage is key!
seasonal_tips.2: March: Watch for new growth. Prune dead branches before leaves emerge, but go easy — it blooms on old wood.
seasonal_tips.3: April: Prime planting time! Water deeply after planting and give it space to spread.
seasonal_tips.4: May: New growth is underway. Water weekly until established, then you can mostly leave it alone.
seasonal_tips.5: June: Buds are forming. Back off the watering — this plant actually prefers it on the dry side.
seasonal_tips.6: July: Bloom time! Watch for the parade of pollinators visiting those fluffy white flower clusters.
seasonal_tips.7: August: Still blooming. Leave spent flowers — they'll become seeds that songbirds love in fall.
seasonal_tips.8: September: Seeds are ripening. Cardinals and sparrows will thank you for leaving them on the plant.
seasonal_tips.9: October: Leaves turning golden yellow. Leave everything standing for winter interest and wildlife shelter.
seasonal_tips.10: November: Going dormant. A thin layer of leaf mulch around the base is all it needs for winter.
seasonal_tips.11: December: Resting underground. A great time to plan which native companions to add next spring!

wildlife.title: Pollinator Hub
wildlife.desc: Attracts bees, butterflies, and beneficial wasps to its broad flower heads

planting_steps: Plant in partial shade to full sun with average to moist soil
Space 18–24″ apart — rhizomes will fill in gaps over time
Cut back by half in early June if you want shorter, bushier plants

notes: A tall prairie perennial with distinctive drooping yellow petals and a raised grayish-brown cone that sways gracefully in the breeze.

fun_facts.text: The drooping yellow petals and raised cone give it a distinctive 'shuttlecock' appearance unlike any other coneflower.
When crushed, the cone smells like anise — earning it the folk name 'prairie coneflower.'
Makes an excellent long-lasting cut flower that can persist in a vase for over a week.

wildlife.title: Native Bees
wildlife.desc: Long bloom season supports many bee species midsummer

wildlife.title: Goldfinch
wildlife.desc: Feeds on seeds from drooping cone heads in fall

wildlife.title: Silvery Checkerspot Butterfly
wildlife.desc: Caterpillars feed on foliage of Ratibida species

planting_steps: Sow seed in fall on soil surface; needs light and cold.
Full sun in average to dry well-drained soil; tolerates clay.
Leave spent cones standing through winter for birds and reseeding.

notes: Tiny star-shaped flowers; good for rock gardens and front of borders

fun_facts.text: Despite its name, it's not a grass at all — it's in the iris family.
Bright yellow star-shaped flowers open only on sunny days and close by evening.

wildlife.title: Small Bee Pollinated
wildlife.desc: Tiny sweat bees and halictid bees visit the cheerful yellow flowers

planting_steps: Plant in full sun with dry to average, well-drained soil
Space 6–8″ apart in rock gardens or meadow edges
Avoid rich soils and overwatering — it prefers lean conditions

notes: A woodland goldenrod with distinctively zigzagging stems and clusters of bright yellow flowers in the leaf axils, perfect for shady gardens.

fun_facts.text: The stems literally zigzag at each leaf node — making it one of the easiest goldenrods to identify even without flowers.
Unlike most goldenrods that need full sun, Zig Zag Goldenrod thrives in deep shade, filling a critical pollinator gap in woodland gardens.
Goldenrods do NOT cause hay fever — they are insect-pollinated; ragweed, which blooms at the same time, is the real culprit.

wildlife.title: Native Solitary Bees
wildlife.desc: Important late-season pollen source in shade

wildlife.title: Monarch Butterfly
wildlife.desc: Nectars on flowers during fall migration

wildlife.title: Chickadees
wildlife.desc: Eat seeds and insects from spent flower stalks

planting_steps: Plant in part to full shade in rich woodland soil.
Space 18 inches apart; ideal for shaded garden edges.
Spreads slowly by rhizomes; minimal maintenance needed.
